does: This paper treats public AI evaluation leaderboards as Bayesian inference problems embedded in incomplete longitudinal archives shaped by reporting conventions and benchmark revisions. The authors analyze repeated snapshots from LiveBench, Open LLM Leaderboard v2, LMArena, GAIA, and tau-bench to demonstrate that a single terminal leaderboard snapshot is compatible with multiple conflicting pre-terminal performance histories. Using synthetic posterior comparisons, they show that standard frontier models fail at synthetic recovery, objective-archive prediction, preference transfer, and uncertainty calibration. They introduce audit gates and decision diagnostics that expose how selection bias in evaluation protocols distorts inference about true capability trajectories, and propose an archive-and-adjudication framework to reconstruct more reliable historical records from selective public evaluations.
connects: This work extends foundational literature on measurement and inference under missing data mechanisms, building on causal inference approaches to selection bias and work on temporal biases in performance benchmarking. It responds to concerns raised in recent AI safety research about how leaderboards can mislead stakeholders through selective reporting and benchmark churn. The paper connects to broader work on evaluation reliability in ML, particularly efforts to understand how benchmark composition and timing interact with model development cycles. It also engages with Bayesian model criticism and posterior predictive checking literature, applying these statistical tools to the specific problem of validating frontier AI capability claims against incomplete archival evidence.
enables: This framework enables future work on principled evaluation protocols that account for reporting biases when making claims about AI capability frontiers. The audit methodology could be adopted by evaluation platforms to issue confidence statements about historical capability trajectories conditional on archive completeness. The work points toward developing formal decision rules for when public leaderboards should be treated as reliable evidence, versus when deeper investigation of missing observations is required. It also sets up opportunities for building better models of how evaluation practice itself evolves, potentially informing design of more robust benchmark rotation and revision schemes. Ultimately, the approach suggests how institutions can reconstruct trustworthy capability timelines from imperfect public records.
